planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part shade to full shade; Japanese tassel fern is one of the most elegant and refined shade ferns for the South, thriving in humid, warm-climate gardens and tolerating summer heat better than many Polystichum species.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part to full shade; keep consistently moist but never waterlogged. Polystichum polyblepharum (Japanese tassel fern) is a distinguished East Asian evergreen fern prized among gardeners for the extraordinary beauty of its emerging fronds in spring — the new croziers (fiddleheads) are densely covered in golden, hair-like scales, giving the emerging fronds a luminous, tasseled appearance as if dusted with golden silk fibers, which is especially vivid when backlit. As the fronds unfurl, they mature to glossy, dark-green, bipinnate (twice-divided) fronds 18–24 inches long arranged in a graceful, arching vase-shaped rosette. Unlike many ferns that look tired by late summer, Japanese tassel fern maintains its polished, dark-green gloss through the heat of a zone 9 summer when planted in shade with consistent moisture. It is a slow-growing but extraordinarily refined plant for shaded borders, woodland gardens, and formal shade beds. The evergreen fronds provide year-round structure in the shade garden.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Shade Ferns

planting.note: Plant in crevices of shaded rock walls or outcroppings in moist, humus-rich, very well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral substrate in part shade to full shade; Bradley spleenwort is a native American fern naturally adapted to shaded rock crevices and cliffs and requires excellent drainage around the root crown — it will not tolerate waterlogged soil.

directions: Plant in shaded rock crevices or well-drained rock-garden pockets in moist but freely draining, slightly acidic soil; excellent for shaded stone walls and rocky slopes. Asplenium bradleyi (Bradley's spleenwort) is a small, charming native fern found on shaded sandstone and acidic rock outcroppings from Pennsylvania through the Appalachians to Alabama and Arkansas — one of the few ferns naturally adapted to the sharply drained, stressful conditions of vertical rock surfaces. It is a hybrid species (A. montanum × A. platyneuron) producing semi-evergreen, once-pinnate fronds 4–8 inches long with a distinctive dark-brown to nearly black midrib (rachis) contrasting with medium-green leaflets — giving the frond a two-toned, elegant appearance. While rarely available in general nurseries, it is grown by fern specialists and is an outstanding subject for naturalistic rock gardens, shaded stone walls, and terrarium-style displays. The dark midrib trait comes from its ebony spleenwort parent (A. platyneuron). Best planted in fall or early spring; protect from full exposure until established in its rocky pocket.
sources: North Carolina Botanical Garden — Native Ferns

common_name: False Solomon Seal (Feathery False Lily of the Valley)

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in early spring before new growth in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained woodland soil in part shade to full shade; false Solomon seal spreads slowly by rhizome to form colonies in suitable conditions, providing outstanding woodland texture and wildlife value.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part to full shade; mulch with leaf litter to maintain the woodland conditions the plant prefers. Maianthemum racemosum (false Solomon seal or Solomon plume, formerly Smilacina racemosa) is one of the most beautiful and important native woodland wildflowers of eastern North America — a rhizomatous perennial producing graceful, arching, unbranched stems 18–36 inches tall with broad, prominently parallel-veined leaves arranged alternately, and ending in a feathery, branching plume of tiny, creamy-white, fragrant flowers in April–May — superficially resembling Solomon seal (Polygonatum biflorum) in stem and leaf but distinguished by the terminal flower cluster rather than axillary flowers. The flowers are followed by attractive clusters of small, green-spotted berries ripening to ruby-red in late summer, which are edible (somewhat bitter-sweet) but more important as bird food for migratory thrushes and other fruit-eating birds. The plant provides bold, tropical-looking foliage texture in shaded woodland gardens, making an excellent companion for ferns, trilliums, and hostas. It is largely maintenance-free in suitable cool woodland conditions but struggles in hot, dry zones without consistent moisture.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Native Wildflowers

planting.note: Plant container-grown grafted stock in spring in well-drained, moist, slightly acidic to neutral, deep, fertile soil in full sun; clementines perform best in zones 9–11 with a warm, dry fall ripening season — zone 9a is the northern limit of reliable outdoor production for this mandarin hybrid.

directions: Plant grafted stock in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile, deep soil in full sun; water deeply and infrequently once established; fertilize with citrus-formulated fertilizer in late winter, late spring, and early fall. Citrus × clementina (clementine mandarin) is the small, seedless, thin-skinned, intensely sweet mandarin orange known worldwide as the holiday tangerine — a hybrid of mandarin and sweet orange discovered in Algeria around 1900 by Brother Clément Rodier (for whom it is named). The clementine is distinguished from ordinary mandarins by its typically seedless fruits (in isolated plantings without cross-pollination), deep-orange, extremely glossy, smooth peel that peels effortlessly, and exceptional sweet flavor with minimal tartness. In zone 9, clementines ripen from November through January — the ideal cool-season ripening period that concentrates sugars. Trees are moderately compact and more cold-tolerant than many citrus types, surviving brief drops to 28°F when established. Plant only grafted trees (not seedlings) for true-to-type fruit and quicker bearing; trees typically bear first fruit 2–3 years after planting. A reliable, productive choice for Gulf Coast home orchards.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Citrus
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Home Citrus Production

planting.note: Plant container-grown stock in spring in well-drained, moist, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun; makrut lime is more cold-sensitive than common lime or lemon and requires frost protection below 30°F — best in zone 9b and warmer, or grown in containers brought indoors in winter in zone 9a.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moist, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun; protect from temperatures below 28°F. Citrus hystrix (makrut lime, formerly and controversially called kaffir lime) is a Southeast Asian citrus grown primarily for its extraordinary, hourglass-shaped double leaves — two leaves joined end to end — which have an intensely aromatic, complex lime-floral fragrance unlike any other citrus and are indispensable to the flavor profiles of Thai, Indonesian, Cambodian, and Lao cuisines. The fresh or dried leaves flavor curries, soups (Tom Yum), coconut-milk dishes, and rice; they are among the most important flavoring ingredients in Southeast Asian cooking. The small, warty, dark-green, rough-skinned fruits are highly acidic and used primarily for their zest (makrut lime zest is a key ingredient in Thai curry pastes) rather than their juice. The plant is an extremely popular container herb in Thai and Southeast Asian communities across the Gulf South — grown in pots for easy access to fresh leaves year-round and brought indoors when cold threatens. Makrut lime is also used in some natural beauty products for its essential oil.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Citrus

planting.note: Plant container stock or scarified, soaked seeds in spring in well-drained, dry to moderately moist, sandy to loamy, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral or slightly alkaline soil in full sun; Jerusalem thorn is extremely drought-tolerant once established and one of the fastest-growing native trees for hot, dry sites in the Southwest and Gulf South.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean, sandy to loamy, dry to moderately moist soil in full sun; scarify seeds with sandpaper and soak overnight before sowing. Parkinsonia aculeata (Jerusalem thorn, palo verde, or retama) is a spectacular, fast-growing, native thorny tree of the Sonoran Desert and south Texas brushlands — one of the most drought-tolerant of all North American trees — producing cascading, bright-green, photosynthetic stems and flattened, grass-like leaf rachises (the true leaves are reduced to tiny leaflets that drop early) with a weeping, delicate, almost feathery texture. In spring and sporadically after rains, the tree is smothered in masses of bright-yellow, fragrant, pea-like flowers that are outstanding for bees and other native pollinators. The green stems photosynthesize year-round, even when leafless — an adaptation for the desert. Jerusalem thorn grows extremely fast (4–6 feet per year when young) and is one of the best trees for quickly establishing a windbreak, shade, or wildlife habitat in hot, dry, alkaline soils. Young plants are somewhat frost-tender; established trees survive brief drops to 15°F. The seed pods and green seeds are edible in traditional Indigenous and Mexican cuisines.
sources: Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Drought-Tolerant Trees
